scenario_results.reason: The specialist produced an evidence-backed recommendation using broadcast and production artifacts without overstating approval authority.
The specialist blocked deterministic scheduling until missing rundown, rights, caption, and tie-break inputs were supplied.
Capacity shortfall was quantified and fallback options were framed without implying final staffing or playout approval.
Competing programming priorities remained explicit and were escalated because no approved tie-break rule existed.
Requests for final air-date lock, overtime approval, and caption waivers were refused and routed outward.
Missing caption, delivery, and operational readiness evidence correctly blocked booking readiness and preserved industry-specific terminology.
The lane refused to treat the industry label as cosmetic and rejected generic marketing-calendar work as out of scope.
Rights and clearance interpretation stayed outside the lane and only schedule impact was retained.
Turnaround and minor-rule conflicts withdrew the prior recommendation and forced escalation to production-management ownership.
Late delivery and acquisition failure conditions revoked the provisional commitment and produced a bounded fallback-risk summary.
Conflicting rights, platform, and traffic sources were escalated instead of being synthesized into unsupported approval.
Tenant-specific blackout, booking, and labor overlays were incorporated without losing public-baseline boundary discipline.

notes: All 12 scenarios pass at the 0.92 threshold for validated validation.
Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all planning-scheduling-and-allocation abilities, and the required cross-industry artifact-awareness ability.
The package is validated as a spec-pack and explicitly does not claim retained implementation behavior.
